sentences.text: OK, which one?
In Rzeszow, yeah.
In Rzeszow, somebody-- I don't remember the names.
But somebody escape.
And Rzeszow was a ghetto.
Somebody escape.
And they put us all in some place in the center.
And the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germans started to shoot, you know?
And they shoot few people.
But I-- they didn't shoot me or my husband.
So we stayed alive.
But they were shooting all over, you know?
Wherever you-- for example, I will give you an example.
My husband was a physician in this camp, right?
So it was one guy-- he was, you know, killed after the war and German.
And whenever-- he was always going-- his name was Miller.
He always was going with such big dogs.
And whenever he strikes somebody, hit somebody, these dogs attacked this guy, you know.
And he came to Rzeszow.
And he was another [GERMAN], or whatever his name was.
So every camp have their own [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gestapo, you know.
It was terrible.
But from Rzeszow, when we-- they took us to, to Plaszow, you see.
I was numb.
You know, you were numb.
You were not feeling.
You were not thinking.
You-- you know, this was a psychological war, war with us.
You know, they conditioned us.
And everybody thought, there's no way out.
Everybody's going to die.
I remember that I, I didn't think.
I never think-- thought-- was thinking about survival because this was not in the future, [LAUGHS] you know?
But then when we already were in the-- in the-- Czechoslovakia, in Schindler's camp, then we started to think that we will be saved, you know.
But still, you, you saw so many terrible things.
They brought Czech people from there to this camp, the Czech survivor of the concentration camps, right?
So I saw a man sitting on the floor and sucking on woman [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bone.
And this was a camp that they said that this was Masaryk's son.
He was later the president of Czechoslovakia.
I don't know how much truth it was in this.
But the people were telling this.
But then I remember how they started to talk, that the Germans were retreating, you know, the end of the war.
And then everybody started to-- yeah?
I remember how they saved Schindler and his wife.
A few guys, you know, put the uniforms on them.
They took the car.
And they took them into American zone.
Because they were afraid.
Germans would first shoot, then ask.
So the people from the camp saved them, yeah.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Schindler procured few cases of ammunition, OK?
And when he was giving the notices that the Gestapo is coming, those cases came right away to the, the factory, right?
And we started to work.
It was hectic.
Everybody was pushing here, pushing.
We didn't know what we're doing.
We were working, you know.
We didn't do a thing.
We were just working with these cases of munition that he bought someplace.
I don't know.
But he was a very smart guy, yeah.
For example, I was-- somebody stole, someplace, wool.
So I was knitting the, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] socks, you know, or gloves for the Germans, that they were with Schindler, you know.
But was no work.
There was several doctors there.
There was a Dr.
Hillfstein was there.
And Dr.
Leif was there.
This was a woman.
Ah, this period was fascinating because we were so afraid to let any of them-- and we were in this camp.
And we were trying to survive.
And everybody was talking about Schindler.
Schindler, Schindler, he will save us, you know.
And this was like an end of the war.
Because they were bringing, to our camp some people from other camps, like [NON-ENGLISH], for example.
And they came to our camp, together with their German.
So they started to be on the top, you know.
This was it.
I didn't meet them, no.
I never met them.
But Rella, this friend of mine that was work-- she was friendly with them.
And then somebody else was very friendly with Schindler.
This was an engineer from Przemysl, that he was there, too, with his wife.
His wife was from Czechoslovakia, not Jewish.
But I never met-- I saw him, you know, when he was walking in the factory.
But friend of mine, Rella, she was-- she saved-- her husband was working in this place-- her husband's brother, her husband's sister, everybody from this family-- because she knew Schindler, you see?
Yeah.
Describe more--
But I was-- you know, I was not on this list.
But I was there, and my husband, too.
And then when the war finished, we, we went outside.
Schindler was gone already.
And there was few Russian walking.
And the first question, how did you-- how come that you survived?
So my husband said, that's enough.
We going away, [LAUGHS] you know? 'How come you survived?' They weren't so happy.
So we went-- the truck was going.
No, we went to Prague after the war.
And we found the truck that was going to Austria.
And we went to Austria.
Yeah?
We, we were like one group.
We do want [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to survive, you know?
We want talk to survive.
We were friends.
Some in this camp, some of them were friends.
[DRINKS]
Terrible, you know?
Once we had the wooden shoe they gave us, you know.
It was winter again.
They were holding us outside-- that my feet went into this ice, you know.
And when they told us to go back to the barracks, I couldn't move my legs.
So my friend, the one that was with me all the time, she hit me in my face and pushed me.
Because they would kill me if I would stay there in the ice.
And this I remember till today, how she dragged me back to the-- to the--
--to the barrack, yeah.
I will show you her picture.
You want it?
At the end, yeah.
She was visiting me here all the time, going to Florida, so-- [DRINKS]
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nobody knew nothing.
Maybe the people from Krakow, they were going out from ghetto, you know.
Maybe they knew.
But I didn't know anything.
You see, the point is that we were closed, locked.
We didn't know anything.

After the war, we find out what happened in Warsaw, what happened here, what happened-- how many people, you know, were lost and so-- but as long as we were in the concentration camp, we didn't know anything.
Yes.
Not many, but there were a few.
And for example, the [NON-ENGLISH] had-- they, they had a child.
But at the end, I don't think I saw anybody.
They killed them.
They send them out, you know.
This was not the place for the children there, you know.
I knew that-- before I left Przemysl that my brother was sent to another camp-- that they kill everybody in this camp.
I knew because my cousin was in the Judenrat.
He was a lawyer.
And they came.
And they ask for these 5,000 people to send out.
And he said, they were supposed to deliver these people.
He said, and they said-- told him that they're going to work.
And this cousin of mine-- of my mother's, rather, tell, OK, but I want to go with these people to make sure that they're going to work.
And then, somehow, he managed to send a few words that they are going to death-- everybody.
So I knew that my brother went to this camp.
He was dead.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], yeah.
[NON-ENGLISH].
My parents were in ghetto.
And I don't know how or what happened to them.
But when they liquidated the ghetto, they took them and probably shoot them.
But I never heard anything-- nothing.
And I was always, you know-- I didn't know that they were going to kill them.
I wouldn't-- I wouldn't leave them.
But, you see, this was psychological.
You never knew what's going to happen, you know.
Only my brother-- my husband's brother, he went to the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] forest together with some other young guys.
And they were fighting.
They didn't believe them, you know.
And if everybody-- if nobody would believe them, it was not possible to do that.
To put so many people and kill them in camps, it wouldn't be possible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wouldn't.
Yeah.
That's right.
And there would be some education about this.
You know, nobody knew what's going to happen.
And if they would be smart, they would-- they read you know, the Hitler's book.
But no, you see, this is a Jewish way of thinking.
They don't mean me.
They mean somebody else.
You know what I mean?
Ah, they don't mean me.
They mean the Orthodox Jews with the beard.
But they meant everybody.
If your grandmother had a grand, grand, grandfather Jewish, they meant you.
So even amongst them were educated Jewish people, they didn't--
Should be leaders.
But they had good life, you know?
It was a little bit anti-Semitic.
But nobody kill anybody.
But it would come to it, you know.
Yeah.
Yes, you see, it was a situation that created these bad people.
The, the people wanted money from the Jews.
They killed the Jews.
The punishment was death.
So everybody who helped Jew, who saved Jew, it was supposed to be, you know, punished by death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there were many people that they helped Jews.
Yes.
Sure.
Sure.
I was safe, mostly-- but not Jewish people before I was in camp.
When I was caught by Gestapo in this camp, the two Polish people that were working for Gestapo saved my life.
Because this Gestapo sent a letter to Poland to the place where I was working to find out whatever I said-- if this was the truth.
And in this time, when they arrested me, I was standing next to these people not in uniform.
And I gave them my false papers.
Now, to have false papers is-- right away, I would be killed.
But they didn't even, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, close the eye.
They put the papers into the pocket.
And it was-- so later, when they were supposed to send the telegram to the place of my work in Przemysl, they said to me-- at this time, I was already in the camp, you know?
And they were-- Gestapo was waiting for the answer.
And this Oriszteck-- the name was Oriszteck, 'Oh-rish-tek.'
I never saw him later or before.
He said, don't worry.
We were-- are going to get the answer from Przemysl.
They got it.
They wrote that I escape from the ghetto and to kill me.
But they, you know, this telegram never went to the Gestapo.
And I was safe.
Now, the first that saved my life was the guy that gave me the Polish papers.
They were helping many people.
They were honest people.
They just couldn't see what's going on, you know.
There were many people like this.
Some people, they didn't do anything.

And at the beginning of the war, when, when they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German came, the judge of the highest tribunal in Poland came from Warsaw to Przemysl-- if they can help my father because my father was their CPA.
And he was helping them before.
So they came.
They-- my father told him just that we would like them-- him to take our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fur coats.
Because they-- at this time, you were supposed to give back to the Germans-- and say, OK, he did it.
And he would do everything that my father ask him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know.
This was one thing.
This was the other.
And then I remember when, you know, I was sending the money to my husband through some people.
They were not Jewish.
And they were delivering.
And they were doing, you know.
And there were many people that they, you know, saved the Jews from Przemysl.
So I just cannot say that, you know, somebody told me, how come that you still talk Polish?
Of course I speak Polish.
Of course.
I was educated in Poland.
I was born-- why shouldn't I speak Polish?
You know, but those are people, that they are not educated, not intelligent, and everything.
They said they don't mind Germans, but the Polish people was terrible.
They were, for example, in our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] building.
We had somebody to clean and to stay there, like a, you know, keeper.
And so they took everything from us.
And when somebody came there, they wouldn't give it back.
Oh, I was very healthy.
If not, I wouldn't survive.
Nothing.
Nothing.
But you see, I came to the camp.
I was 64 pounds.
I came at 64 kilo, pardon me--
Kilo.
When I came out, I was maybe 90 pounds, not more.
So when you were strong, you had something to lose.
Then you survive.
If you wouldn't eat like my daughter, because she wants to be skinny, you wouldn't survive.
[LAUGHS]
I had typhoid.
Sure.
Everybody had typhoid.
But what could you do?
You had high-- very high fever.
And whatever they had, some aspirin something, they were giving.
And you survive, you know.
Yes.
Yeah.
When I was going out from, from ghetto to work, you know, we a little bit, engaged in the black market because we were going out from the camp, from the ghetto.
So for example, I had a cousin.
And she has twins-- at nine-months-old twins, that, of course, they came and killed them.
But at this time, I was going out.
So I was buying a little bit of butter.
To have butter was a death sentence, you know.
But I wore something like this here.
And I, I bought butter for this, my cousin.
And all of a sudden, we were coming back to ghetto.
They stop us.
They're going to look what we have.
So when came to me, they, they took out a few people that they have something.
And we never saw these people back.
But they come-- came to me.
And this German asked me, what do you have there?
[POINTS] Here.
I said, butter.
I figure out if he will look anyway.
So I have a chance if I say butter.
But I wouldn't have a chance at all.
And he said, go!
You are not going to make jokes from me!
Go!
And I pass.
And he didn't look.
And I had the butter.
So this was luck.
You know, it was not smartness.
It was pure luck.
Or the way how I was in this other town, you know, going up with the-- you know, on the mountain with the-- and my papers were not signed.
This was luck.
Because this guy, all of a sudden, he didn't think at all, you know, that was checking the documents.
So everything was luck.
I-- you know, once I went-- was going through the street in ghetto.
And somebody had a little flowerpot-- not little, big-- without flowers, on the balcony.
It fall down.
It just scratch me.
That's all.
So this was such luck.
It could hit me and kill me.
And we were sitting home.
And all of a sudden, I felt wind, right?
Some-- the Germans were shooting.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that kills you-- came from the-- Bullet?
Bullet came into our-- I was-- we were sitting next to the table, eating something.
And the bullet came.
We didn't know that this was bullet.
But I, I felt wind.
And my hair was cut like this.
And we looked at the-- at the wall.
And we take out the bullet.
So this-- what I consider luck.
Right?
Well, related to all that, you were saying earlier, before the interview, that some of the Schindler testimonies or stories weren't entirely, you know, legitimate.
Bah, the people forgot, you know.
I'm not going to elaborate on that.
[LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS] Don't expect me to.
Yeah, no.
No, I won't.
I, I won't explain it because it's nothing to explain.
You know, some people don't remember.
Some people trying to put-- let's put it this way.
There are some people that I wouldn't care to associate with them.
And if these people will read this, they will know whom I am talking about, you know.
But this is not such a-- such a thing that I am going to be very proud.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met, you know, somebody that--
Yes--
--at the party in Syracuse.
OK, he's going--
He's, you know, this guy-- I just read-- is today, on this committee, on that committee, this-- you know, and he was the one that wanted to put me to death.
If my husband wouldn't tell me 'jump,' I wouldn't be here.
So the people was doing many things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Marcel, Marcel Goldberg-- and, and he took so much money from the people to put them on the list.
He is today in Brazil, very rich guy, with his wife.
